overview_cards.title: Access control
overview_cards.body: ACL checks, ACL categories, key/channel/database rules, failed-auth accounting, and ACL LOG entries are useful production behavior. They also add request lifecycle work that a password-only fast path does not pay.

overview_cards.title: Observability
overview_cards.body: Slow log, commandstats, MONITOR, client info, and tracking metadata make a server easier to operate. Benchmarks should say whether those hooks are implemented and enabled.

overview_cards.title: Durability and replication
overview_cards.body: AOF/RDB/snapshot, replication offsets, WAIT, WAITAOF, and maxmemory enforcement change what a write must account for before a reply can be emitted.

overview_cards.title: Batching shape
overview_cards.body: Pipeline depth rewards products that classify and batch simple single-key commands. A command-at-a-time compatible executor can be correct while still losing headline P64 throughput.

cells.Dragonfly: Supported, with historical release notes showing ACL rollout and later key support.
cells.Lux: Password auth documented; Redis ACL users/rules not observed in reviewed tree.
cells.FeOx server: Password auth documented; command metadata has ACL categories, but Redis ACL users/rules not observed.

cells.ArilKV: Supported: ACL LOG tests and command declaration are present.
cells.Dragonfly: Supported in recent releases; verify exact version under test.
cells.Lux: Not observed in reviewed public tree.
cells.FeOx server: Not observed in reviewed public tree.

cells.ArilKV: Supported: SLOWLOG command declaration and runtime tracking.
cells.Valkey: Supported through SLOWLOG; Valkey also documents COMMANDLOG as an alternative.
cells.Dragonfly: Dragonfly has slowlog-related implementation history; verify exact command behavior for the target version.
cells.Lux: Not observed as Redis SLOWLOG in reviewed public tree.
cells.FeOx server: Not observed as Redis SLOWLOG in reviewed public tree.

cells.ArilKV: Supported through INFO commandstats, client metrics, CLIENT INFO/LIST.
cells.Dragonfly: Broad Redis-compatible operational surface; verify the fields used by your tooling.
cells.Lux: Monitoring is advertised for cloud, but Redis commandstats parity was not observed in local tree.
cells.FeOx server: INFO counters and CLIENT commands are documented; full commandstats parity was not observed.

cells.Dragonfly: Release notes mention initial client tracking and MONITOR fixes; verify target version.
cells.Lux: Lux documents KSUB/KUNSUB as a different key-change subscription model, not Redis CLIENT TRACKING.
cells.FeOx server: Not observed in reviewed public tree.

cells.ArilKV: Replication and WAIT/WAITAOF command surface are present; persistence commands are part of the server surface.
cells.Valkey: Full Redis-derived replication and persistence surface.
cells.Dragonfly: Snapshots and replication are documented; exact WAIT/WAITAOF parity should be tested per version.
cells.Lux: Automatic snapshots, WAL, and tiered storage are documented; Redis replication parity was not observed.
cells.FeOx server: Persistent data path is documented; Redis replication and WAIT/WAITAOF parity were not observed.

cells.ArilKV: Owner-thread execution is default; current hot path still executes simple commands mostly command-by-command.
cells.Valkey: Single event-loop execution unless configured otherwise; highly optimized baseline.
cells.Dragonfly: Pipeline squashing and multi-threaded shared-nothing architecture are part of the product story.
cells.Lux: Docs explicitly describe batching consecutive same-shard commands under one lock.
cells.FeOx server: Source shows inline SET/GET fast paths in the connection loop.

guidance: Exact server versions or commit hashes, plus container image digests when Docker is used.
Whether ACLs are disabled, password-only, or full Redis ACL users/rules with ACL LOG.
Whether slow log, commandstats, MONITOR, CLIENT TRACKING, and client metadata are active.
Persistence and replication mode: no durability, snapshot/WAL/AOF, replica configured, WAIT/WAITAOF tested or not.
Pipeline strategy and client shape: pipeline depth, clients, benchmark threads, cluster mode, key distribution, and hit ratio.
CPU and isolation details: core pinning, performance-core selection on heterogeneous CPUs, Docker limits, and whether client/server share cores.
